Unlike your knees or shoulders, these joints are held tightly together by strong, thick bands of tissue called ligaments and are only meant to move a tiny fraction of an inch to absorb shock when you walk, run, or jump.

When these joints are put under uneven stress, they can become jammed and locked up, or the ligaments can stretch out, making the joint too loose. Whether the joint is moving too much or not enough, the result is sudden, intense inflammation. This throws off the balance of your entire pelvis, triggering severe muscle spasms and sending a sharp, stabbing pain right over the dimples of your lower back and deep into your buttocks.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, stabbing pain directly over the "dimples" at the very bottom of your lower back.

  • A deep, dull ache in your buttocks that often travels down the back of your thigh, but rarely goes past your knee.

  • A sudden jolt of pain when transferring your weight, such as getting out of a car, standing up from a chair, or climbing stairs.

Common causes:

  • A sudden slip or fall, such as landing hard on your backside after slipping on an icy driveway or missing a step on a staircase.

  • Uneven repetitive stress, like favoring one leg due to an old injury, carrying a heavy child strictly on one hip, or walking with an uneven stride.

  • Pregnancy, as your body releases hormones that naturally relax and loosen the pelvic ligaments to prepare for childbirth, causing the SI joints to become temporarily unstable.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is perfectly suited for SI joint dysfunction because it directly addresses the mechanical alignment of your pelvis. Through precise, gentle adjustments, we can unjam a "stuck" SI joint, restoring its normal, healthy, shock-absorbing movement. If the joint is too loose, we use specific stabilization techniques and targeted exercises to help your muscles support the pelvis properly. This balanced approach quickly relieves the uneven stress, allowing the sharp pain and tight muscle spasms to fade away.
definition: Sacroiliac (SI) joint dysfunction happens when the strong joints connecting the base of your spine to your pelvis move too much or too little, causing sharp pain in your lower back, hips, and buttocks.

name: Knee Osteoarthritis
description:
Imagine the ends of your knee bones are covered in a slick, protective layer of tissue called cartilage. This smooth layer acts like Teflon, allowing your bones to glide effortlessly past each other when you walk, bend, or jump. With knee osteoarthritis, this protective cushioning slowly wears away, thins out, and becomes rough over time.

As the cartilage breaks down, the bones in your knee start to rub closer together. Your body tries to fix this unwanted friction by creating extra joint fluid, which causes swelling, or by growing tiny new bumps of bone, called bone spurs. All of this extra friction and swelling makes the knee joint feel stiff, incredibly painful, and much harder to move smoothly.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, aching pain in the knee that usually gets worse after walking, standing, or being physically active.

  • Severe stiffness in the joint, especially when you first wake up in the morning or after sitting in a chair for a long time.

  • A gritty, grinding, or popping sensation and sound when you bend or straighten your leg.

Common causes:

  • Natural aging and the accumulation of everyday "wear and tear" on the joint over decades of use.

  • Past knee injuries, such as an old sports injury or torn ligament, which change how the knee moves and speeds up the breakdown of the joint.

  • Carrying extra body weight, which puts a heavy, constant, and multiplying stress on the knees with every single step you take.

How chiropractic can help:

While we cannot regrow lost cartilage, chiropractic care is highly effective at improving how your knee joint functions. By gently adjusting the knee, hip, and ankle, we ensure your entire leg is correctly aligned. This shifts your body weight and takes the uneven, grinding pressure off the most damaged parts of your knee. We also work on relaxing the surrounding muscles, giving the joint more space to move freely and significantly reducing your daily pain and stiffness.
definition: Knee osteoarthritis is a "wear and tear" condition where the smooth, protective cushioning inside your knee joint gradually breaks down, causing stiffness, grinding, and pain.

name: Spondylolisthesis
description:
Your spine is made up of a stack of sturdy bones that are held perfectly in line by small joints and strong bands of tissue. Sometimes, due to a stress fracture, heavy wear and tear over the years, or a sudden injury, the stabilizing structures in the back of the spine give way. When this happens, a spinal bone can physically slide forward out of alignment.

This slippage most commonly happens in the lower back. If the bone only slips a tiny bit, you might just feel a deep, stubborn ache. But if it slides far enough, it can narrow the tunnel where your spinal cord and nerves live, pinching them and sending intense pain or numbness down your legs. To protect the unstable area, your lower back and hamstring muscles often become incredibly tight, creating a stiff, guarded feeling every time you try to move.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, continuous ache in your lower back that feels like a severe muscle strain that simply won't go away.

  • Pain that gets noticeably worse when standing for a long time or leaning backward, but magically feels better when you sit down or lean forward.

  • Severe tightness in your hamstrings (the back of your thighs) that makes it hard to bend over and changes how you walk.

  • Numbness, tingling, or a shooting pain traveling down one or both legs if the slipped bone begins crowding a nerve.

Common causes:

  • Repeated stress or a tiny fracture in the bone from activities or sports that require a lot of backward bending, like gymnastics, weightlifting, or football.

  • Normal aging and wear-and-tear that causes the joints and shock-absorbing cushions in your spine to break down and lose their grip on the bones.

  • A sudden trauma or high-impact injury, like a hard fall onto your back or a car accident, that forcefully pushes the bone out of place.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care focuses on safely stabilizing the spine and taking pressure off the overworked areas. Because the slipped bone is unstable, we do not use forceful pressure directly on it. Instead, we use very gentle, targeted techniques to improve the movement of the joints above and below the slip, reducing the overall mechanical stress on your lower back. We also use specific therapies to relax your tightly guarded muscles and teach you targeted core exercises to build a "natural back brace" of muscle, keeping the spine safe and secure.
definition: Spondylolisthesis happens when one of the bones in your spine slips forward out of its proper position and rests directly on the bone below it.

name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
description:
Your shoulder is a highly mobile ball-and-socket joint that is enclosed in a stretchy, flexible sleeve of connective tissue called the shoulder capsule. When you develop a frozen shoulder, this capsule becomes heavily inflamed, thickens, and develops tough bands of scar tissue. Because the capsule is no longer stretchy, it physically binds and squeezes the shoulder joint, completely taking away its ability to move freely.

This condition usually progresses through three distinct, frustrating stages. First, the shoulder becomes incredibly painful as the capsule begins to inflame (the "freezing" stage). Next, the pain may start to lessen, but the shoulder becomes completely stuck and rigid (the "frozen" stage). Finally, over many months, the capsule slowly begins to loosen up and mobility returns (the "thawing" stage). The entire process severely limits your ability to do normal, everyday tasks and requires patience and targeted care to resolve.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, severe ache in the shoulder that is often worst at night, making it incredibly difficult to find a comfortable sleeping position.

  • A progressive, physical inability to lift your arm up, reach across your body, or reach behind your back to grab a wallet or hook a garment.

  • A feeling of the joint being completely locked in place, where even if someone else tries to gently lift your arm for you, the shoulder physically will not move past a certain point.

Common causes:

  • Prolonged immobility, such as keeping your arm in a sling or resting it too much after a minor injury, surgery, or fracture, which allows the capsule to stiffen up.

  • Underlying metabolic or hormonal conditions, specifically diabetes or thyroid disorders, which can make your body's tissues more prone to severe inflammation and scarring.

  • Age and gender factors, as this condition most commonly and mysteriously affects women between the ages of 40 and 60.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care focuses on safely preserving and restoring as much movement as possible while your shoulder goes through the healing phases. By using gentle, specific adjustments to the shoulder joint, collarbone, and upper spine, we ensure that the surrounding mechanics are perfectly aligned to take excess stress off the frozen capsule. We also utilize targeted soft tissue therapies to help actively break up the restrictive scar tissue, manage the pain, and speed up the "thawing" phase so you can regain your range of motion faster.
definition: Adhesive capsulitis, commonly known as frozen shoulder, is a condition where the flexible tissue surrounding your shoulder joint severely thickens and tightens, causing intense pain and a near-complete loss of movement.

name: Thoracic Disc Herniation
description:
Your spine is made up of stacked bones called vertebrae, and between each one is a flexible pad called a disc. These discs act like little jelly donuts, with a tough outer ring and a soft, gel-like center. Sometimes, due to everyday wear and tear or a sudden injury, the tough outer layer tears, and the soft "jelly" center pushes out. When this happens in your mid-back (the thoracic spine), it is called a thoracic disc herniation.

Because the space around the spinal cord in your mid-back is quite small, that bulging disc material can easily press against delicate nerves or the spinal cord itself. This pressure creates inflammation and sends pain signals not just in your back, but often wrapping around your rib cage or even into your stomach and chest area.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, shooting pain that wraps around your ribs or chest, sometimes mimicking heart or digestion issues.

  • A deep, aching pain in the middle of your back, right between or just below your shoulder blades.

  • Numbness, tingling, or a "pins and needles" feeling that travels around your torso or down into your legs.

Common causes:

  • Lifting something heavy with a twisting motion, putting sudden, forceful pressure on the mid-back discs.

  • Gradual wear and tear over time, causing the discs to dry out, weaken, and become more prone to tearing.

  • A sudden impact or trauma, such as a fall, sports injury, or car accident that forcefully jolts the mid-back.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps relieve the pressure on the injured disc by gently improving the alignment and movement of the surrounding spinal joints. By taking the physical stress off the affected area, we can help reduce nerve irritation, decrease muscle spasms, and create a better environment for the disc to heal naturally.
definition: This condition occursA thoracic disc herniation happens when one of the soft, shock-absorbing pads in your mid-back bulges or breaks open, pressing on nearby nerves and causing pain. when a disc in the middle back bulges or ruptures, potentially pressing on nearby nerves.

name: Thoracic Strain / Sprain
description:
Your mid-back (the thoracic spine) is supported by a complex network of muscles and tough, fibrous bands called ligaments. A "strain" happens when the muscles or the tendons attaching them to the bone are stretched too far or torn. A "sprain" occurs when the ligaments that connect your spinal bones together are overstretched.

In both cases, the injury causes your body's natural defense system to kick in, flooding the area with inflammation. This inflammation acts like a warning signal, causing the injured muscles to tightly lock up in protective spasms to prevent you from moving and doing more damage. While this stiffness is meant to act as a natural splint to protect you, it leaves your mid-back feeling intensely tight, sore, and difficult to move.

Common symptoms:

  • A sudden, sharp pain in your mid-back immediately following a lifting, reaching, or twisting motion.

  • Severe muscle stiffness and cramping between or just below the shoulder blades.

  • Pain that gets noticeably worse when you take a deep breath, cough, sneeze, or try to turn your torso.

Common causes:

  • Lifting heavy objects incorrectly, especially if you forcefully twist your body while picking something up.

  • Sudden, awkward movements, such as swinging a golf club, throwing a ball, or jerking your body to brace for a fall.

  • Chronic poor posture that slowly overstretches and weakens the mid-back muscles over time, until a minor, everyday movement causes them to finally give out.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps relieve a mid-back sprain or strain by gently restoring proper movement to the spinal joints surrounded by the injured tissues. By ensuring the bones are properly aligned and moving freely, we remove mechanical stress from the healing muscles and ligaments. This signals your body to relax the painful muscle spasms, improves blood flow to flush out the inflammation, and speeds up your natural healing process.
definition: A thoracic sprain or strain is an injury to the muscles, tendons, or ligaments in your mid-back, usually caused by overstretching or a sudden, awkward twisting motion.

name: Thoracic Facet Syndrome
description:
Your mid-back, known as the thoracic spine, is made up of stacked bones. On the back of each of these bones are small, hinge-like joints called facet joints. These joints guide your spine's movement, allowing you to smoothly twist, bend, and stand up straight. They are lined with slippery cartilage and surrounded by a protective capsule to keep them gliding easily.

However, when these tiny joints are subjected to too much stress—often from poor posture, repetitive twisting, or just sleeping awkwardly—they can become jammed, swollen, and highly irritated. Because the muscles in your back are directly connected to the movement of these joints, this sudden inflammation often triggers surrounding muscle spasms. This creates a painful cycle where the joints are locked, the muscles are tight, and simple movements like taking a deep breath or turning your torso become incredibly painful.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, catching, or stabbing pain right between the shoulder blades, especially when trying to twist your torso or lean back.

  • A feeling of severe stiffness or being "locked up" in the mid-back, making it difficult to stand up perfectly straight.

  • Discomfort or a sudden, sharp ping of pain when taking a very deep breath, coughing, or sneezing.

Common causes:

  • Postural strain from slouching over a desk, phone, or steering wheel for long periods, which puts constant, heavy pressure on these small mid-back joints.

  • A sudden, awkward twisting motion, like turning quickly to reach for something in the backseat of your car, which can sprain the joint.

  • Sleeping in an unsupportive position that leaves your mid-back twisted or unsupported throughout the night.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is perfectly suited for facet syndrome because it goes straight to the source of the locked joint. Using very precise, gentle adjustments, we can restore normal gliding motion to the stuck facet joints in your mid-back. This targeted movement immediately relieves the pressure on the joint, allows the painful inflammation to drain away, and signals the surrounding tight muscles to relax so you can twist and move freely again.
definition: Thoracic facet syndrome happens when the small, stabilizing joints in your mid-back become stiff, inflamed, or irritated, causing a sharp or catching pain between your shoulder blades.

name: Cervical Sprain/Strain
description:
The word "cervical" simply refers to your neck. A "strain" happens when a muscle or a tendon (the cord that connects muscle to bone) is overstretched or torn. A "sprain" is very similar, but it happens to a ligament, which is the tough, elastic band of tissue that connects your bones together. When your neck is forcefully jerked or pushed beyond its normal, comfortable range of motion, these delicate soft tissues get damaged.

In response to this injury, your body acts quickly to protect your neck. It floods the area with inflammation and triggers your neck muscles to tightly clench or go into spasm. This acts as an internal splint to prevent you from moving and causing more damage, but it also creates the intense stiffness, soreness, and pain you feel when trying to turn your head.

Common symptoms:

  • A steady, aching pain that sharply increases when you try to move your head or look over your shoulder.

  • Severe stiffness that makes it difficult to turn or tilt your neck, almost as if it is locked in place.

  • Hard, tender muscle spasms or knots in the back of your neck and upper shoulders.

Common causes:

  • Whiplash from a car accident, where the sudden impact throws your head violently forward and backward.

  • Sports injuries, rapid twists, or sudden falls that unexpectedly jar the neck.

  • Sleeping in a highly awkward position or suddenly jerking your head to look at something during daily activities.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is highly effective for recovering from neck sprains and strains because it helps restore proper alignment and movement to your spine. By performing gentle, targeted adjustments, we take the mechanical pressure off your injured soft tissues. This allows the protective muscle spasms to finally relax, improves healthy blood flow to clear out inflammation, and accelerates the natural healing process so you can regain a pain-free range of motion.
definition: A cervical sprain or strain is an injury to the soft tissues in your neck—such as the muscles, tendons, or ligaments—usually caused by a sudden movement or overstretching.

name: Subacromial bursitis
description:
Inside your shoulder joint, there is a small, slippery sac of fluid called a bursa. Its main job is to act like a tiny shock absorber or gliding cushion between the bones of your shoulder and the delicate tendons that help you lift your arm. When you repeatedly lift your arms over your head or experience a sudden shoulder injury, this tiny cushion gets squeezed and irritated.

In response to this friction, the bursa fills up with extra fluid and swells, becoming inflamed. Because the space inside your shoulder is already very narrow, this swollen sac gets painfully pinched against the bone every time you try to raise your arm. This creates a frustrating cycle of pinching and swelling, making simple movements like putting on a shirt or reaching for a high shelf feel incredibly stiff and painful.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, aching pain in the front or side of the shoulder that turns into a sharp pinch when lifting the arm overhead.

  • Pain that wakes you up at night, especially if you accidentally roll over onto the affected shoulder while sleeping.

  • A feeling of weakness and restricted motion when trying to reach behind your back, wash your hair, or put on a jacket.

Common causes:

  • Repeated overhead motions, like painting, swimming, or throwing a ball, that constantly squeeze and irritate the bursa.

  • Poor posture, such as slouching, which rolls the shoulders forward and permanently narrows the space where the bursa sits.

  • A direct bump or fall onto the side of the shoulder that suddenly crushes and inflames the cushioning sac.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps relieve subacromial bursitis by restoring the proper alignment and mechanics of your shoulder joint, collarbone, and upper back. By gently adjusting these areas, we can actively create more space in the shoulder joint, which instantly stops the bones from pinching the swollen bursa. This allows the inflammation to drain away naturally and takes the pressure off the joint so your shoulder can glide pain-free once again.
definition: Subacromial bursitis is a painful condition where a small, fluid-filled cushioning sac in your shoulder becomes swollen and inflamed, making it difficult to lift your arm.

name: Iliopsoas Tendinopathy/Bursitis
description:
The iliopsoas (pronounced ill-ee-oh-SO-as) is the major muscle group that connects your lower back to the top of your thigh bone. It acts as your primary "hip flexor," meaning it is the powerful muscle responsible for lifting your knee toward your chest and helping you walk or run. When this muscle gets overworked or chronically tight, the tendon that attaches it to your leg can become strained, developing tiny, painful tears.

Right next to this tendon is a small, fluid-filled sac called a bursa, which acts as a natural gliding pad to prevent the tendon from rubbing directly against the hip bone. When the tight tendon scrapes heavily across the bone, both the tendon and the bursa can become severely irritated and swollen. This double-layered inflammation makes the front crease of your hip feel tight, achy, and painful, especially when you try to stretch the front of your leg or bring your knee up to walk.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep ache or sharp pain in the groin or front of the hip that often radiates down the front of the thigh.

  • A painful snapping, clicking, or catching sensation deep in the hip when moving your leg forward or standing up.

  • Severe stiffness and pain that gets noticeably worse when trying to stand up straight after sitting for a long period of time.

Common causes:

  • Prolonged sitting, which forces the hip flexor muscles to stay in a shortened, tight position for hours, causing them to lock up and pull hard on the tendon.

  • Repetitive overuse from high-demand activities like distance running, soccer, cycling, or gymnastics that constantly strain the front of the hip.

  • A sudden, violent overstretching movement, like slipping backward, lunging too far, or missing a step, which abruptly yanks the tendon.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care focuses on relieving the mechanical stress that is causing the muscle to tighten up in the first place. By gently adjusting your lower back, pelvis, and hips, we ensure your body is properly aligned, which stops your leg from pulling unevenly. We also use targeted soft tissue massage and stretching techniques to physically release the chronically tight iliopsoas muscle, giving the tendon and bursa the slack they need to stop rubbing so they can finally heal.
definition: Iliopsoas tendinopathy or bursitis is an inflammatory condition that causes a deep ache and stiffness in the front of your hip and groin, making it painful to lift your leg or stand up completely straight.

name: Trochanteric Bursitis
description:
On the outer, bony point of your hip, there is a small, jelly-like sac called a bursa. This bursa acts as a natural cushion or gliding pad, preventing the thick tendons and muscles of your leg from rubbing directly against the hard hip bone when you walk or move. When these muscles become too tight or are overworked, they pull and scrape heavily across this bursa.

Over time, this constant, repetitive friction causes the bursa to become severely irritated, swollen, and inflamed. Instead of providing a smooth gliding surface, it turns into a painful, swollen knot on the outside of your hip that flares up whenever you move your leg, stand for too long, or even try to lie on that side in bed.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp or deep aching pain on the outside of your hip that may travel down the outside of your thigh.

  • Severe tenderness when you press on the outside of your hip or try to sleep on that side at night.

  • Pain that worsens when walking up stairs, getting up from a deep chair, or after walking for a long time.

Common causes:

  • Repetitive friction from activities like distance running, cycling, or walking up steep hills.

  • Biomechanical imbalances, such as having legs of slightly different lengths, flat feet, or poor posture, which put uneven stress on the outside of the hip.

  • Tight hip and thigh muscles that act like a stretched rubber band, constantly snapping across the bursa and irritating it.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care relieves the uneven pressure causing the bursa to inflame. By gently adjusting the pelvis, hips, and lower back, we restore proper mechanical alignment so your body weight is distributed evenly. We also use soft tissue therapies to loosen the tight muscles on the side of your hip and thigh, stopping the constant friction so the inflamed bursa has the physical space it needs to finally heal.
definition: Trochanteric bursitis is a painful inflammation of the small, fluid-filled sac (bursa) that sits on the bony outside point of your hip.

name: Childcare With Upper Back /  Neck  Pain
description: Feeding, carrying, and comforting your child often means hours spent hunched forward, which can lead to nagging upper back and neck pain. Whether you're nursing, bottle-feeding, or soothing a toddler on your shoulder, poor posture can quickly add up to discomfort. Fortunately, with a few mindful changes to your routine and body mechanics, you can ease the strain and support long-term spinal health. Follow the information link for simple, effective tips to help reduce upper back and neck stress during everyday childcare tasks.

name: Degenerative Disc Disease
description:
Your spine is made of stacking bones, and between each bone is a soft disc that acts like a tiny jelly doughnut. These discs give your spine its flexibility and absorb the impact of your daily movements. As we age, or due to past injuries, these discs can lose their water content, flatten out, and become brittle. This natural wearing-down process is called Degenerative Disc Disease. As the discs lose their thickness, there is less padding between the bones of your spine. This lack of cushioning can cause the spine to become stiff, irritate nearby nerves, and cause the surrounding muscles to ache.

Common symptoms:

  • A dull, constant ache in your lower back or neck.

  • Pain that flares up and gets worse when you sit for a long time, bend, lift, or twist.

  • Feeling stiff and "creaky" in the morning, but feeling a bit better once you get up and move around.

Common causes:

  • The natural aging process, which causes the discs to lose their hydration and thin out over time.

  • Years of everyday wear and tear from heavy lifting, physically demanding jobs, or high-impact sports.

  • Previous injuries to the spine, like a car accident or a fall, which can jumpstart the wearing-down process earlier than normal.

How chiropractic can help: Chiropractic care cannot reverse the aging of a disc, but it can significantly improve how your spine functions. By gently adjusting the spine, a chiropractor helps restore movement to stiff joints, takes unnecessary pressure off the worn-out discs, and reduces nerve irritation. This helps you move more comfortably and prevents the joints from wearing down as quickly.
definition: A condition where the soft, shock-absorbing pads between the bones in your spine slowly dry out and wear down over time.

name: Pregnancy & Postpartum Chiropractic Care Plan
description:
The journey of pregnancy and the postpartum period brings incredible, rapid changes to your body. As your baby grows, your center of gravity dramatically shifts, placing intense new stress on your lower back and altering your natural biomechanics. Our clinical goal during this phase is to support your changing body, safely relieve joint strain, and reduce the common discomforts associated with carrying a child.

Through gentle, specialized adjustments and targeted therapies, we focus on maintaining optimal pelvic alignment and nervous system function. We also place a strong emphasis on balancing the musculature of your posterior chain to comfortably support the added weight without developing unhealthy movement compensations. After delivery, our care seamlessly transitions to focus on your postpartum recovery—helping your loosened joints restabilize, restoring proper biomechanics, and ensuring your body heals beautifully so you can focus on enjoying your new baby.
definition: This plan helps your body adapt to the physical changes of having a baby. It keeps your pelvis aligned and your back strong so you can stay comfortable during pregnancy and recover faster afterward.

name: Bedwetting (Nocturnal Enuresis)
description:
While it can be incredibly frustrating and embarrassing, bedwetting is rarely bad behavior or simple laziness. It almost always boils down to a communication problem in the body. While sleeping, the brain and the bladder are supposed to "talk" to each other. When the bladder gets full, it should send a clear, strong signal to the brain to either wake the person up or tell the bladder muscles to hold it tight. In cases of bedwetting, this signal gets delayed, crossed, or ignored entirely during deep sleep, leading to nighttime accidents.

Common symptoms:

  • Consistently waking up with wet pajamas and bedsheets.

  • Sleeping incredibly deeply, to the point where a full bladder doesn't trigger waking up.

  • Feelings of embarrassment, anxiety, or avoiding social events like sleepovers.

Common causes:

  • A delayed or weak connection between the brain and bladder signals during the night.

  • A bladder that hasn't fully matured yet and is too small to hold a whole night's worth of urine.

  • Underlying issues like constipation, which can cause the bowel to physically press against the bladder and reduce its capacity.

How chiropractic can help:

The nerves that control the bladder exit the spine in the lower back and pelvis. If those specific spinal joints are restricted or misaligned, it can create "static" on the nerve lines, interfering with that crucial brain-to-bladder communication. Gentle, kid-friendly chiropractic adjustments help remove this nerve interference, allowing the brain and bladder to talk clearly again, which often helps kids naturally start having dry nights.
definition: A condition where a child (or sometimes an adult) accidentally empties their bladder while sleeping, well past the age when they are typically expected to stay dry at night.

name: Acromioclavicular (AC) Joint Sprain/Dysfunction
description:
Your shoulder is an incredibly complex area, and right at the top of it is a small but vital connection called the acromioclavicular (AC) joint. This is the exact spot where the end of your collarbone attaches to the roof of your shoulder blade. It acts as a critical pivot point, allowing you to seamlessly raise your arm above your head, lift heavy objects, or reach across your chest. When this joint is injured—either from a sudden fall, heavy lifting, or repetitive wear and tear—the strong, rubber-band-like ligaments that hold these bones together can get overstretched, slightly torn, or the joint itself simply becomes physically "stuck" out of its normal alignment.

When this happens, your body rushes inflammation to the area to protect it like an internal cast. This swelling makes the very top of your shoulder feel incredibly tender, stiff, and painful. Every time you try to lift your arm, use a tool, or simply roll over in bed, those injured ligaments are pulled tight, or the stuck joint grinds, resulting in a sharp spike of pain that stops you in your tracks and severely limits your arm movement.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, localized pain right at the bony bump on the top of your shoulder, especially when trying to lift your arm high or reach across your body.

  • A noticeable bump or visible swelling at the end of the collarbone that is extremely tender and sensitive when you press on it or wear a heavy strap.

  • A deep, aching stiffness in the shoulder that makes it intensely painful to sleep on that side or support your body weight.

Common causes:

  • Falling directly onto the outside of the shoulder or bracing yourself with an outstretched hand, which is a frequent occurrence during fast-paced, reactive sports like pickleball.

  • Heavy, awkward lifting that forces the shoulder joint out of its safe alignment, such as moving heavy clinic machinery or hoisting a large cast iron griddle.

  • Repetitive reaching and pulling motions, such as yanking the pull-cord to start stubborn lawn equipment or doing overhead DIY mechanical repairs.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is highly effective for AC joint issues because it focuses on safely restoring the proper mechanical alignment of your entire shoulder complex. By performing very gentle, specific adjustments to the collarbone, shoulder blade, and upper back, we can safely "un-jam" the restricted joint. This instantly removes the abnormal stress and pulling on the healing ligaments, allows the localized swelling to drain away naturally, and helps you regain full, pain-free movement in your arm.
definition: An AC joint sprain or dysfunction is a painful injury or "jamming" that occurs right at the bony bump on the very top of your shoulder, where your collarbone meets your shoulder blade.

name: Glenohumeral Joint Dysfunction
description:
Your shoulder is an incredibly mobile joint, technically called the glenohumeral joint. It works like a golf ball sitting on a tee, where the "ball" at the top of your arm bone rests inside a shallow "socket" on your shoulder blade. When everything is perfectly balanced, this joint allows you to move your arm in almost a complete circle.

However, everyday wear and tear, poor posture, or minor injuries can cause this ball-and-socket mechanism to slightly shift out of its ideal alignment or become "stuck." This is what we mean by "dysfunction." Because the joint is no longer gliding smoothly, your body has to force the movement. This extra friction and mechanical stress quickly irritate the surrounding muscles, ligaments, and joint capsule, leading to localized inflammation, a feeling of deep stiffness, and sharp pain when you try to lift or rotate your arm.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, dull, and constant ache inside the main shoulder joint that often feels worse at the end of the day or when trying to sleep.

  • A "stuck" or severely stiff sensation, making it difficult to reach behind your back, put on a coat, or lift your arm straight up.

  • A sharp, pinching catch when you attempt to move your arm outward or rotate it in certain directions.

Common causes:

  • Poor Posture: Slouching forward at a computer or steering wheel rolls your shoulder blades forward, which physically pushes the ball-and-socket joint out of its natural alignment over time.

  • Repetitive Strain: Performing the same arm movements over and over, such as painting, throwing, or heavy lifting, which slowly jams the mechanics of the joint.

  • Sudden Impact or Guarding: A minor slip, fall, or sudden jerk of the arm can slightly misalign the joint, causing your muscles to lock up defensively to protect it.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is incredibly effective for glenohumeral joint dysfunction because it directly addresses the mechanical "jam" in the joint. By using gentle, precise adjustments to the shoulder, collarbone, and upper back, we can safely guide the ball back into proper alignment with the socket and restore its smooth, gliding motion. This immediately takes the abnormal pressure off your surrounding muscles and tissues, reduces the painful inflammation, and helps your shoulder move freely and comfortably again.
definition: Glenohumeral joint dysfunction is a condition where the main ball-and-socket joint of your shoulder gets jammed, stiff, or stops moving smoothly, causing pain and restricting your arm movement.

name: Post-Accident Instructions
description:
If you've recently been in a motor vehicle accident, it's common to feel sore, stiff, or even a bit shaken up—sometimes symptoms don’t show up for hours or even days. Following these guidelines can help you recover safely and monitor for signs of injury.

Things to Do:

  • Rest and Hydrate: Give your body time to recover. Drink plenty of water and get adequate rest in the first few days.

  • Apply Ice: Use an ice pack (15–20 minutes at a time) to reduce inflammation, especially in the neck, shoulders, or lower back.

  • Move Gently: Light movement helps circulation. Short walks and gentle stretching can prevent stiffness—but avoid strenuous activity.

  • Follow Care Recommendations: If you've started chiropractic care, attend all scheduled visits and follow your provider's at-home instructions.

Things to Avoid:

  • Don’t Ignore Pain or Stiffness: These can worsen without treatment. Report any new or increasing symptoms to your healthcare provider.

  • Avoid Heavy Lifting or High-Impact Activities: These can aggravate underlying injuries.

  • Don’t Sit for Long Periods: Prolonged sitting can lead to increased back and neck pain. Change positions often.

  • Avoid Over-the-Counter Medications Without Guidance: Some medications may mask important symptoms that help us diagnose your condition accurately.

 Symptoms to Watch For:

Call your healthcare provider or seek immediate medical attention if you experience:

  • Increasing pain, numbness, or tingling in arms, legs, hands, or feet

  • Severe or worsening headaches

  • Dizziness, balance problems, or confusion

  • Vision changes or sensitivity to light

  • Difficulty sleeping or concentrating

  • Swelling or bruising that worsens over time
definition: Guidelines on managing your activity levels and monitoring symptoms to ensure your body heals correctly and to prevent further irritation after an injury.

name: Rotator Cuff Syndrome/Tendinopathy
description:
Your shoulder is an incredibly flexible joint that relies on a dedicated team of four muscles and their connecting tendons—collectively known as the rotator cuff. These tendons act like strong ropes that securely hold your arm bone directly inside the shallow shoulder socket, allowing you to lift, reach, and rotate your arm in almost any direction.

When you frequently lift heavy items, make repetitive overhead movements, or strain your arms in awkward positions, these "ropes" can experience tiny micro-tears. Because tendons naturally have a poor blood supply, they heal very slowly. Your body responds to this constant strain by flooding the area with inflammation, causing the tendons to swell up. When these swollen tendons get pinched between the bones of your shoulder as you lift your arm, it creates a frustrating cycle of deep pain, limited movement, and a feeling of weakness that makes using your arm incredibly difficult.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, dull ache deep inside the shoulder joint that often feels worse at night, especially if you roll over and sleep on that side.

  • A sharp, catching pain or "twinge" when you try to reach up high, out to the side, or behind your back to grab something.

  • A noticeable loss of strength in your shoulder, making your arm feel heavy and weak when trying to lift everyday objects.

Common causes:

  • Repetitive overhead arm motions, such as serving or swinging a paddle during racket sports like pickleball, or frequently casting a fishing line.

  • Awkward reaching and forceful pulling, like maneuvering heavy hand tools while doing DIY mechanical repairs on equipment.

  • Chronic postural strain, such as slouching forward for long hours while doing focused computer work or full-stack web development, which rolls the shoulders forward and crowds the tendons.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps relieve rotator cuff pain by fixing the mechanical alignment of your shoulder, upper back, and neck. By using precise, gentle adjustments, we ensure your shoulder blade and collarbone are moving properly, which instantly creates more space in the joint and stops the bones from pinching your inflamed tendons. We also use targeted soft tissue therapies to break down painful scar tissue in the shoulder muscles, allowing the overworked rotator cuff to finally relax, heal, and regain its normal strength.
definition: Rotator cuff syndrome is a painful shoulder condition that happens when the group of muscles and tendons responsible for keeping your arm securely in its socket become overworked, irritated, or inflamed.

name: Sciatica
description:
The sciatic nerve is the longest and thickest nerve in your body. It is made up of several nerve roots that start in your lower back, join together in your pelvis, and run all the way down the back of each leg to your feet. It acts like a major electrical cable, sending messages and feeling back and forth between your brain and your legs.

When something in your lower back or pelvis—like a bulging spinal pad, a stiff joint, or a severely tight muscle—presses against or pinches this major nerve, it interrupts those signals. This compression acts like stepping on a garden hose, causing the nerve to become inflamed and send frantic distress signals. Instead of normal feeling, you experience intense pain, burning, tingling, or numbness traveling anywhere along the path of that nerve, from your buttock down to your toes.

Common symptoms:

  • A sudden, electric or shooting pain that starts in your lower back or buttock and zips down the back of one thigh and calf.

  • Numbness, tingling, or a frustrating "pins and needles" feeling in your foot or toes, much like your leg has fallen asleep and won't wake up.

  • A feeling of heaviness or unexpected weakness in the affected leg, making it difficult to stand up, walk normally, or push off your foot.

Common causes:

  • A herniated or bulging disc in the lower back, where the soft cushion between your spinal bones pushes outward and presses directly against the sciatic nerve roots.

  • A severely tight muscle deep in your buttocks (called the piriformis muscle) that spasms and clamps down on the sciatic nerve as it passes underneath.

  • Natural wear and tear over time that causes the passageways in your lower spine to narrow, creating a "traffic jam" that crowds and squeezes the nerves.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care is highly effective for sciatica because it focuses on finding exactly where the nerve is being pinched and relieving that physical pressure. By using gentle, targeted adjustments, we restore proper alignment and movement to the joints in your lower back and pelvis. This takes the mechanical stress off the area, signals tight muscles to relax, and gives the inflamed sciatic nerve the space it needs to heal, quickly reducing the shooting pain down your leg.
definition: Sciatica is a condition where a pinched or irritated nerve in your lower back or hip causes a sharp, shooting pain that travels down the back of your leg.

name: Chronic Low Back Pain
description:
While sudden pain acts like a loud alarm bell warning you of a new injury, chronic pain is more like a faulty smoke detector that simply won't turn off. Your lower back carries the majority of your body's weight. When the joints, muscles, or spinal discs endure long-term wear and tear, or when an old injury doesn't heal correctly, the area remains in a constant state of low-grade irritation.

Over time, this lingering stress forces the surrounding back muscles to constantly work overtime to try and protect the spine. Your body adapts by creating tight, stiff muscle patterns to guard the area. This creates a frustrating cycle: the persistent stiffness limits your ability to move normally, and that lack of movement creates even more stiffness, making it difficult and exhausting to get through your normal daily activities.

Common symptoms:

  • A constant, dull, and heavy ache across your belt line that never seems to fully go away.

  • Severe morning stiffness that makes it hard to stand up straight or put on your shoes when you first wake up.

  • A deep, tired sensation that flares up into a sharper pain after standing for long periods or sitting at a desk.

Common causes:

  • Unresolved past injuries, like an old back sprain or auto accident, that healed with stiff, inflexible scar tissue instead of healthy muscle.

  • Years of repetitive strain or poor posture, which gradually wears down the cushioning discs and irritates the spinal joints.

  • Weak core and abdominal muscles that fail to properly support the spine, putting all the physical pressure directly on the bones and ligaments.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps break the frustrating cycle of chronic pain by addressing the underlying mechanical stiffness rather than just masking the symptoms. Through gentle, specific adjustments, we restore proper motion to the joints in your lower back that have been locked up for months or years. This relieves the physical pressure on tired muscles, helps break down old scar tissue, and resets the nervous system to finally turn down those lingering pain signals.
definition: Chronic low back pain is an ongoing, stubborn ache or stiffness in your lower spine that lasts for more than three months, often lingering long after an initial injury should have healed.

name: Gluteal Tendinopathy
description:
The muscles in your buttocks are incredibly important for everyday movements. They help you walk, climb stairs, and keep your pelvis perfectly balanced when you stand on one leg. These large muscles attach to the bony bump on the outside of your hip using tough, rope-like bands of tissue called tendons.

When these tendons are forced to work too hard or are pulled at uneven angles over a long period, they can develop tiny micro-tears. Because these tendons have a slow blood supply, they struggle to heal quickly. Over time, the repeated stress causes the tendon to break down, becoming chronically inflamed, stiff, and deeply painful. This makes everyday activities that put weight on your hip, or simply trying to sleep on your side, very uncomfortable.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, dull ache on the outside of your hip that occasionally travels down the outside of your thigh.

  • Sharp pain that flares up when climbing stairs, walking up a hill, or standing up after sitting for a while.

  • Sleep-disrupting tenderness that makes it nearly impossible to lie comfortably on the affected hip at night.

Common causes:

  • A sudden increase in physical activity, like taking up a new walking routine or pushing yourself too hard on a hike before your body is ready.

  • Weakness in the hip and buttock muscles, which forces the tendons to take on far more of your body weight than they are designed to handle.

  • Postural habits that place uneven pulling stress on the hip, such as constantly standing with your weight shifted onto one leg or sitting with your legs tightly crossed.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care focuses on taking the uneven, damaging pulling pressure off those struggling tendons. By gently adjusting your pelvis, hips, and lower back, we restore proper mechanical alignment and balance to your lower body. We also use targeted soft tissue therapies to release tight, overworked buttock muscles, giving the tendons the physical slack they need to stop fraying so they can finally rest and heal naturally.
definition: Gluteal tendinopathy is a painful condition where the strong bands of tissue connecting your buttock muscles to your outer hip bone become irritated, worn down, and sore.

name: Home Care Tips for Achilles Tendinitis
description:
Things You Can Do at Home:

  • Rest Your Leg
     Limit walking, running, or activities that stress the back of your ankle. Let your tendon recover by avoiding high-impact movement.

  • Apply Ice
     Use ice packs on the tendon for 15–20 minutes, 2–3 times a day, especially after activity, to reduce swelling and pain.

  • Stretch Gently
     Light calf stretches and towel stretches (while seated) can help improve flexibility and ease tension on the tendon. Don’t stretch into pain.

  • Wear Supportive Shoes
     Use shoes with good arch support and cushioning. Avoid walking barefoot or in flat shoes like flip-flops.

  • Use a Heel Lift
     A small heel cushion or lift in your shoe can reduce strain on the Achilles tendon by slightly shortening its stretch while walking.

  • Massage and Self-Care
     Gentle massage to the calf muscles and tendon area may reduce tightness and promote blood flow.

Things to Avoid:

  • Running or jumping while the tendon is still sore

  • Sudden increases in exercise or intensity

  • Flat shoes with no support

  • Stretching too aggressively
definition: A combination of gentle calf stretching, specific resting positions, and targeted icing to reduce tension and soothe the thick tendon at the back of your heel.

name: Cervical Myofascial Pain Syndrome
description:
The word "cervical" simply means your neck, and "myofascial" refers to your muscles and the thin, slippery layer of connective tissue (fascia) that covers them. When you have this syndrome, it means the muscles in your neck and upper back have been stressed or overworked for so long that they have formed tight, rope-like bands that refuse to relax.

Within these tight bands of muscle are tiny, highly sensitive knots known as "trigger points." When these knots are pressed, or even when you just move your neck normally, they don't just hurt right where they are—they can also send a deep, aching pain spreading out to your head, shoulders, or arms. This constant muscle tension limits your movement and can make your neck feel exhausted and heavy by the end of the day.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, steady ache in the back or sides of your neck that doesn't seem to go away with rest.

  • Tender, hard knots in your neck and upper shoulder muscles that send pain elsewhere (like up into your head) when you press on them.

  • Stiffness and a feeling that your head is too heavy to hold up, especially after sitting at a desk or driving for a long time.

Common causes:

  • Poor posture, such as slouching over a computer or constantly looking down at a smartphone, which puts non-stop strain on the muscles supporting your head.

  • Emotional stress and anxiety, which often cause people to unconsciously shrug their shoulders and clench their neck muscles tightly for hours at a time.

  • Repetitive movements or heavy lifting that continually overworks the exact same muscles in your upper back and neck without giving them time to recover.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care offers excellent relief by addressing both the painful muscle knots and the underlying joint issues that cause them. Through gentle adjustments, we ensure the bones in your neck are moving properly, which takes the mechanical stress off your overworked muscles. We can also use hands-on techniques to physically break up those tight trigger points, allowing the muscle fibers to finally relax, improve healthy blood flow, and heal naturally.
definition: Cervical myofascial pain syndrome is an ongoing, deep ache and stiffness in your neck muscles, usually caused by tight, highly sensitive spots called trigger points.

name: Patellar Tendinopathy (Jumper's Knee)
description:
Your patellar tendon acts like a strong, thick rubber band that works with the muscles in the front of your thigh to help you straighten your leg. This is the crucial tendon that allows you to kick, run, walk up stairs, and jump. When you repeatedly place heavy demand on this tendon—especially through jumping, sudden stops, or hard landings—it can develop tiny micro-tears.

Instead of healing properly, repeated stress causes the tissue to become inflamed, weakened, and extremely painful. Over time, the tendon loses its natural elastic stretch and becomes stiff and swollen, making every movement that bends your knee under your body weight feel like a sharp ache.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, burning pain right below your kneecap, especially when jumping, landing, or running.

  • Stiffness and a deep ache in the knee after sitting with your legs bent for a long time.

  • Tenderness and mild swelling localized directly on the tendon beneath the kneecap.

Common causes:

  • Sudden spikes in physical activity, like playing a lot of basketball or volleyball over a short period of time.

  • Tight leg muscles, especially the hamstrings or quadriceps, that constantly pull on the knee joint.

  • Playing or training on hard, unforgiving surfaces like concrete or asphalt without adequate shock absorption.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care helps by looking at how your entire lower body absorbs shock. By ensuring your feet, ankles, hips, and lower back are correctly aligned, we take the uneven, heavy stress off your knees. We also use targeted soft tissue techniques to loosen the tight thigh muscles that are constantly yanking on the patellar tendon, giving the micro-tears a chance to properly rest and heal naturally.
definition: Patellar tendinopathy, often called "jumper's knee," is a painful irritation of the thick band of tissue that connects your kneecap to your shinbone.

name: Active Release Technique (ART)
description:
When muscles or tendons are overused or injured, your body heals them by creating dense, sticky scar tissue. Unfortunately, this scar tissue can bind up your muscles, making them feel weak, tight, and painful. It can even trap delicate nerves, leading to numbness or a tingling sensation. We recommend the Active Release Technique because it is specifically designed to locate and eliminate this painful scar tissue at its source.

During an ART session, we will use our hands to apply precise pressure to the stiff or injured area while simultaneously guiding you to move that specific body part. This combination of hands-on pressure and your active movement creates a gentle tension that breaks apart the sticky scar tissue. By freeing up these restricted tissues and trapped nerves, ART instantly improves your flexibility, relieves your pain, and helps your body heal correctly.
definition: Active Release Technique (ART) is a specialized massage that uses your own movement to break up tough scar tissue in your muscles. It helps release trapped nerves and tight muscles so you can move smoothly and without pain.

name: Achilles Tendinopathy
description:
The Achilles tendon is the thick, powerful band of tissue at the back of your lower leg that helps you push off the ground when you walk, run, or jump. When this tendon is asked to do more work than it is accustomed to, or if there is excessive tension pulling on it from tight calf muscles, it can develop tiny micro-tears.

Unlike a sudden tear, tendinopathy is usually a "wear and tear" problem. Instead of simply being inflamed, the tendon fibers become irritated and break down over time, leading to a thickened, painful, and stiff tendon that struggles to stretch and move properly.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep ache or sharp pain at the back of your heel and lower leg that gets worse when walking, running, or climbing stairs.

  • Severe stiffness and tenderness in the back of your ankle when you first wake up in the morning.

  • Noticeable thickening or a small, tender bump on the tendon right above the back of your heel.

Common causes:

  • A sudden increase in the intensity or duration of physical activities, such as taking up a new running routine or playing sports with quick stops and starts like pickleball.

  • Having extremely tight calf muscles that constantly pull and yank on the Achilles tendon with every step.

  • Wearing unsupportive shoes that lack proper heel elevation or shock absorption, forcing the tendon to work harder.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care addresses the mechanical stress that is overloading the tendon. By gently adjusting the joints in your ankle and foot, we can correct any misalignments that are causing you to walk unevenly. We also use hands-on techniques to release tight, knotted calf muscles, taking the constant tension off the Achilles tendon so the micro-tears can finally heal.
definition: Achilles tendinopathy is an overuse injury that causes pain, stiffness, and swelling in the large tendon that connects your calf muscle to your heel bone.

name: Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)
description:
Your hip is a large ball-and-socket joint designed to move smoothly. In a healthy hip, the ball fits perfectly inside the socket and glides effortlessly. With FAI, extra bone grows on either the ball, the socket, or both. This changes the natural shape of the bones, meaning they no longer fit together like a perfect puzzle piece.

Instead of gliding smoothly, these mismatched bony edges grind and pinch against each other when you move your leg. Over time, this constant, hard friction can pinch and damage the protective rubbery seal (the labrum) and the smooth cartilage inside your hip. This leads to deep aching, sharp catching pains, and a severe stiffness that limits your ability to move your leg comfortably.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, aching pain in your groin or the front of your thigh, especially after sitting for a long time or walking.

  • A sharp, stabbing, or catching sensation deep in your hip when you turn, pivot, or squat down.

  • A feeling of severe stiffness or a physical "block" that makes it difficult to put on your shoes, cross your legs, or bring your knee up toward your chest.

Common causes:

  • Bone Shape and Growth: Being born with or developing slight abnormalities in how your hip bones grow during childhood, resulting in extra bone on the ball or socket.

  • Repetitive Stress: Participating in sports that require deep squatting or heavy twisting motions (like hockey, soccer, or martial arts), which forcefully bumps the hip bones together repeatedly.

  • Wear and Tear: The natural thickening of bone in response to stress over time, creating small bony bumps that disrupt the smooth mechanics of the joint.

How chiropractic can help:

While chiropractic care cannot magically shave away extra bone, it is highly effective at improving how your hip and pelvis move to minimize the painful pinching. By gently adjusting the pelvis and lower back, we can ensure your body is perfectly balanced. This improves the overall mechanics of the joint, shifts pressure away from the pinched areas, and relaxes the tight surrounding muscles, allowing you to move with far less friction and pain.
definition: Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI), often called hip impingement, is a condition where the bones of your hip joint don't fit together perfectly, causing them to rub and pinch against each other during movement.

name: Metatarsalgia
description:
The ball of your foot is the padded area right behind your toes. It acts as a major shock absorber, taking on a huge amount of pressure every time you push off the ground to walk, run, or jump. When this area is subjected to repetitive stress, or if the mechanics of your foot are slightly off, the delicate bones in this area (called metatarsals) and the surrounding tissues become severely bruised and inflamed.

Instead of a smooth, cushioned step, the structural arch across the front of your foot may flatten out, causing the bones to press directly down into the sensitive padding. This results in a persistent, nagging pain that can make walking barefoot or wearing unsupportive shoes nearly impossible without significant discomfort.

Common symptoms:

  • A sharp, aching, or burning pain in the ball of your foot, right behind the toes.

  • A sensation that feels exactly like you have a pebble in your shoe or a folded-up sock stuck under your foot.

  • Pain that worsens when you stand, run, or flex your feet, and improves when you sit down and take the weight off your foot.

Common causes:

  • Wearing poorly fitted footwear, especially high heels or shoes with a narrow toe box that squeeze the front of your foot and force your weight forward.

  • Participating in high-impact sports that involve a lot of running or jumping, like tennis, distance running, or high-intensity interval training.

  • Having a foot shape that puts extra pressure on the ball of the foot, such as naturally high arches or a second toe that is longer than the big toe.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care focuses on the alignment of the many small bones in your foot. By applying gentle, precise adjustments, a chiropractor can restore the natural arch across the ball of your foot, lifting those compressed bones off the painful, inflamed tissues. We also work on releasing tight muscles in the foot and calf to ensure your weight is distributed evenly, taking the direct, pounding pressure off the injured area so it can heal naturally.
definition: Metatarsalgia is a condition where the ball of your foot becomes incredibly painful and inflamed, often feeling like you have a bruised pad or are stepping on a rock.

name: Chronic Neck pain
description:
Unlike a sudden injury that flares up and heals in a few weeks, chronic neck pain is a persistent issue that sticks around. This usually happens when the delicate structures in your neck—the joints, muscles, and ligaments—are dealing with long-term stress, repetitive strain, or slow wear-and-tear. Over time, these areas become constantly irritated.

In response to this ongoing irritation, your body keeps the surrounding neck and shoulder muscles constantly tight to try and protect the area. This creates a frustrating cycle of stiffness, reduced movement, and ongoing discomfort. Because these muscles are working overtime day in and day out, your neck can start to feel exhausted, heavy, and intensely achy, especially toward the end of a long day.

Common symptoms:

  • A constant, dull ache or throbbing sensation deep in the neck, often spreading into the upper back and shoulder blades.

  • Persistent stiffness that makes it difficult to fully turn your head, look up, or comfortably check your blind spot while driving.

  • A feeling of a "heavy head," where your neck muscles feel completely exhausted just from holding your head upright all day.

Common causes:

  • Years of poor posture, such as continuously looking down at a smartphone or slouching over a computer screen, which puts immense daily stress on the neck.

  • An older, unresolved injury—like a minor whiplash from a past car accident or an old sports injury—that altered how your neck joints move.

  • Gradual wear-and-tear on the spinal joints over time (often called osteoarthritis), which can cause the joints to become stiff, inflamed, and less flexible.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care tackles chronic neck pain by breaking the long-standing cycle of joint stiffness and muscle tension. Through gentle, specific adjustments, we help restore proper, healthy movement to spinal joints that have been stuck or moving poorly for months or even years. This takes the constant pressure off irritated nerves, allows chronically tight muscles to finally relax, and improves local blood flow to help your neck heal naturally.
definition: Chronic neck pain is an ongoing, stubborn ache or stiffness in your neck that lingers for more than three months, often making your neck feel tired and tight every day.

name: Electrical Muscle Stimulation (EMS)
description:
When you suffer an injury, your muscles often tighten up to protect the area, leading to painful spasms, stiffness, and inflammation. We recommend Electrical Muscle Stimulation (EMS) to help break this frustrating cycle. During the treatment, we place small, comfortable sticky pads on your skin around the injured area. These pads deliver mild, soothing electrical pulses that feel like a light tingling or a gentle, rhythmic massage.

These therapeutic impulses work in a few amazing ways. First, they fatigue the muscle just enough to force it to relax, which effectively stops the spasming. Second, they help flush out swelling and bring fresh, healing blood into the area. Finally, the mild tingling actually blocks pain signals from reaching your brain while encouraging your body to release its own natural painkillers, called endorphins. This therapy provides excellent, immediate relief and sets the perfect stage for a smoother, faster overall recovery.
definition: Electrical Muscle Stimulation (EMS) is a therapy that uses tiny, painless electrical pulses to gently massage your muscles. This helps stop muscle spasms, reduces swelling, and blocks pain signals so you can feel better faster.

name: Relieve Nerve Compression and Irritation
description:
Your nervous system is like the electrical wiring for your entire body, carrying vital messages between your brain and every single muscle, organ, and tissue. When a spinal bone misaligns or a disc bulges out of its normal position, it can physically pinch or irritate the delicate nerves exiting your spine. This compression interrupts those important messages, often leading to sharp, shooting pain, uncomfortable numbness, or a frustrating "pins and needles" sensation.

Our primary clinical focus is to carefully relieve this mechanical pressure. Through precise, targeted chiropractic adjustments, we work to restore proper motion and healthy alignment to your spine, physically moving the joint off the irritated nerve. Removing this interference is crucial because it not only stops the immediate, radiating pain but also allows your nervous system to heal. Once the pressure is gone, your body can communicate properly again, ensuring you can return to your active life feeling strong and capable.

Therapeutic Benefits of Relieving Nerve Compression and Irritation:

  • Eliminates Radiating Pain: Stops the sharp, burning, or shooting pain that travels away from your spine and down into your arms, hands, legs, or feet.

  • Restores Normal Sensation: Resolves uncomfortable numbness, tingling, or "pins and needles" feelings caused by blocked or scrambled nerve signals.

  • Improves Muscle Strength: Re-establishes clear, uninterrupted communication between the nerve and the muscle it controls, eliminating the weakness often associated with a pinched nerve.

  • Promotes Nerve Tissue Healing: Removes the mechanical friction and chemical inflammation, allowing the delicate nerve fibers the space and peace they need to recover fully.

  • Enhances Overall Function: Ensures your brain and body can communicate without interference, improving your overall physical coordination, health, and vitality.
definition: Our goal is to gently take the pressure off your pinched nerves so they can send clear signals again. This stops the sharp pain, numbness, and tingling you might be feeling in your arms, hands, legs, or feet.

name: Auto Accident & Personal Injury Recovery
description:
An auto accident, fall, or sudden impact can cause hidden damage to your muscles, ligaments, and joints—even if you don't feel the pain right away. When your body experiences trauma like whiplash, it rushes to stabilize the area. While this is a natural defense mechanism, it can lead to the buildup of rigid scar tissue, chronic stiffness, and ongoing discomfort if the injuries are not treated correctly. This recovery plan is specifically engineered to address the unique trauma of sudden impact injuries.

We recommend this targeted plan to guide your body through the most crucial phases of the healing process. First, we focus on gently reducing initial inflammation and calming your nervous system. Next, we work to restore normal joint motion and flexibility. Finally, we strengthen the affected areas so they heal correctly and securely. By carefully managing your recovery now, we help prevent these sudden injuries from turning into lifelong problems, giving you back your comfort, mobility, and peace of mind.
definition: This plan helps your body heal properly after the sudden jolt of a car crash or injury. It guides your recovery step-by-step so you can heal fully without leaving behind lasting stiffness or chronic pain.

name: Cervical Traction
description:
When the joints in your neck become compressed from poor posture, daily stress, or an injury like whiplash, the discs between your spinal bones can bulge and pinch sensitive nerves. This often leads to sharp neck pain, tension headaches, or a frustrating tingling sensation traveling down your arms and into your hands. We recommend cervical traction to safely and gently relieve this built-up pressure at the source.

During this therapy, a specialized device or the doctor's hands will provide a highly controlled, gentle upward stretch to your neck. This creates a slight separation between your cervical vertebrae, taking the painful pinch off your nerves and allowing stiff, guarded neck muscles to fully relax. By opening up these joint spaces, cervical traction also helps draw fresh, healing fluids back into your spinal discs, speeding up your recovery and bringing you soothing, lasting relief.
definition: Cervical traction is a gentle therapy that carefully stretches your neck to take pressure off your spinal discs and nerves. This helps relieve neck pain, headaches, and numbness so you can move your head comfortably again.

name: Pediatric & Developmental Care Plan
description:
Growing up is hard work! From the physical process of birth to the countless tumbles of learning to walk, and later, the heavy backpacks and sports of the school years, a child's spine goes through a lot. This care plan is specifically designed to support your child's rapidly developing nervous system and musculoskeletal structure. We use incredibly gentle, safe, and specialized adjustments tailored specifically for little bodies to remove any nerve interference and keep their spine aligned.

By starting care early, we are proactively setting a foundation for lifelong health. We monitor their biomechanical development to ensure they are hitting physical milestones without developing poor posture or unhealthy movement compensations. Our clinical goal is to help your child sleep better, recover from minor bumps and bruises faster, and grow into a strong, healthy, and resilient adult.
definition: This plan uses very gentle techniques to help your child's spine and nervous system grow healthy and strong. It ensures their little body works perfectly as they learn to crawl, walk, and play.

name: Cupping Therapy
description:
When your muscles get too tight or injured, blood flow can slow down, causing painful knots, stiffness, and a buildup of inflammatory waste in your tissues. We recommend Cupping Therapy to act like a "reverse massage." Instead of pressing down on your sore, sensitive muscles, this therapy gently lifts them up to relieve deep pressure and create vital space under your skin.

During your session, we place specialized cups on your skin to create a gentle, comfortable vacuum effect. This suction immediately draws fresh, oxygen-rich blood into the area while helping to flush out the stagnant fluid that causes your pain. It is an incredibly soothing and effective way to relax deep muscle spasms, improve your flexibility, and help your body heal from the inside out so you can get back to your active life.
definition: Cupping therapy is a relaxing treatment that uses gentle suction to pull up your skin and tight muscles. This helps loosen stubborn knots, brings fresh blood to the area, and speeds up your body's natural healing process.

name: Sleep Posture Modification
description:
Sleep posture modification involves making intentional changes to your sleeping position and pillow setup to ensure your spine remains in a neutral, supported alignment throughout the night. We often specifically recommend avoiding stomach sleeping, which forces your neck to twist sharply to one side and puts excessive strain on your lower back for hours at a time. By transitioning to back or side sleeping with the proper pillow support, you allow your muscles and ligaments to fully relax and recover while you sleep.

We highly recommend this home care strategy because your body does the majority of its healing and cellular repair during deep sleep. If you are sleeping in an awkward, twisted position, you are unknowingly re-injuring the exact tissues we are working so hard to heal in the clinic. Correcting your sleep posture is a simple but profound way to protect your chiropractic adjustments, eliminate morning stiffness, and ensure you wake up feeling refreshed and ready to move.

Therapeutic Benefits of Sleep Posture Modification:

  • Eliminates Morning Stiffness: Prevents the tight, achy muscles and locked joints that occur after holding your spine in an awkward, twisted position all night.

  • Protects Your Neck and Lower Back: Removes the severe rotational stress on your cervical spine and the harsh compression on your lumbar spine caused by stomach sleeping.

  • Accelerates Healing: Allows your muscles, ligaments, and discs to fully decompress and receive maximum blood flow during your body's natural nighttime repair cycle.

  • Improves Sleep Quality: Reduces tossing, turning, and waking up in pain, helping you achieve the deep, restorative sleep necessary for overall health.

  • Supports Chiropractic Adjustments: Acts as a nightly brace, reinforcing the healthy alignment and mobility restored during your in-office treatments.
definition: Changing how you sleep, like switching from your stomach to your side or back, helps keep your spine straight while you rest. This prevents you from waking up with a stiff neck or an aching back and lets your body heal.

name: Care Phase:  Acute Care Plan
description:
The Acute Pain Relief Phase is the crucial first step in your chiropractic journey. When you first visit our clinic, you are likely experiencing immediate, often intense discomfort caused by a recent injury, a sudden flare-up, or a specific movement that pushed your body too far. During this phase, our primary objective is to rapidly reduce your pain, lower the inflammation, and relieve the severe muscle spasms that are acting like a painful internal splint.

We achieve this by utilizing gentle, targeted chiropractic adjustments alongside soothing therapies like ice application or soft tissue work. We aren't focusing on heavy strengthening or long-term structural correction just yet; right now, it is all about putting out the fire. By calming your nervous system and taking the mechanical pressure off your sensitive joints, we help you transition out of a state of constant physical distress and prepare your body for the deeper healing to come.
definition: Our first goal is to stop your pain and help you feel better as quickly as possible. We focus on calming down your angry muscles and joints so you can get back to your normal life.

name: Navicular Dysfunction
description:
Your foot is built a lot like a suspension bridge, and the highest point of that bridge is supported by a small, boat-shaped bone called the navicular. This bone acts as a structural keystone, locking everything together so your foot can support your body weight and absorb the heavy shock of walking or running. When this bone gets jammed, shifts out of its normal position, or stops moving smoothly, the structural "bridge" of your foot begins to fail.

Because the bone is no longer supporting the foot correctly, the surrounding tendons and ligaments have to work much harder to hold up your arch. This constant, unnatural straining leads to inflammation, swelling, and a deep, nagging ache in the middle of your foot. It essentially leaves your foot feeling unstable and makes it very uncomfortable to stand or walk for any extended length of time.

Common symptoms:

  • A deep, dull ache on the inner side or top of your foot, right over the highest part of your arch.

  • A feeling of weakness or instability, as if your foot is flattening out and rolling inward every time you step down.

  • Tenderness and mild swelling in the midfoot that makes wearing tight, laced shoes very uncomfortable.

Common causes:

  • Repeated impact from activities like running, jumping, or long-distance walking on hard, unforgiving surfaces.

  • Wearing flat, unsupportive footwear that fails to provide a cushion, forcing the arch of the foot to collapse heavily under your body weight.

  • A sudden twist or sprain, like stepping awkwardly off a curb or uneven ground, that physically jams the small joints in the middle of the foot.

How chiropractic can help:

Chiropractic care directly addresses the mechanical problem by unlocking the jammed navicular bone. Using very specific, gentle adjustments to the midfoot, we can shift this crucial bone back into its proper alignment. This restores the structural strength of your arch, immediately taking the excessive pulling tension off the surrounding tendons and ligaments so your foot can function normally and pain-free.
definition: Navicular dysfunction is a painful condition where a key supportive bone in the middle of your foot drops or loses its normal movement, causing your arch to weaken and hurt.

name: Spinal Manipulative Therapy (Chiropractic Adjustment)
description:
Spinal Manipulative Therapy, commonly known as a chiropractic adjustment, is a safe and highly effective treatment designed to restore proper movement to joints that have become restricted or locked up. During this procedure, your chiropractor uses their hands or a specialized instrument to apply a quick, precise, and gentle force to the affected area. You may hear a slight "pop" during the process, which is simply the release of tiny gas bubbles inside the joint, much like opening a can of soda.

We highly recommend this treatment because when your joints aren't moving properly, it creates a chain reaction: it causes muscle tension, local inflammation, and nerve irritation, which ultimately leads to pain. By restoring normal motion to your spine, adjustments help take the physical pressure off of those sensitive tissues and nerves.

This therapy does more than just provide relief from your immediate discomfort. By removing these roadblocks in your musculoskeletal system, an adjustment allows your nervous system to communicate properly and your body's natural healing processes to take over, helping you get back to feeling and moving your absolute best.
definition: A chiropractic adjustment is a careful, targeted movement applied to your spine or joints. It helps fix stiff areas so your body can move better, feel less painful, and heal faster.

name: Return to Pain-Free Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
description:
Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) are the essential, everyday tasks that you need to perform to live independently and comfortably. When you are suffering from a musculoskeletal injury, simple actions like tying your shoes, reaching for a high shelf, or sitting at your desk can suddenly become frustrating and painful obstacles.
